design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate side-scrolling levels, defeat enemies to gain evolution points (meat drops), spend points upgrading 8 body sections to adapt to environmental challenges and progress
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Adaptation pressure: player must evolve their creature to survive hostile environments and stronger enemies across five geological eras, balancing offensive upgrades against survivability
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Witnessing and directing the evolution of life from fish through dinosaurs to humans; creating a unique creature and guiding it toward paradise (Eden); competing with other lifeforms for supremacy
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Rejects traditional RPG progression (no leveling, no items). Instead uses organism-specific body part upgrades affecting both stats and appearance. Story frames evolution as guided competition under Gaia's watch, with external malevolent force (Mars crystals) corrupting natural selection. No game over—only resurrection with EVO point penalty, keeping narrative agency with player

difficulty.lives_system: No traditional lives. Character defeated = Gaia resurrects player on map screen with ~50% EVO point deduction, preserving progression while penalizing failure
difficulty.continues_system: Implicit continue on resurrection; no hard game over state exists
difficulty.checkpointing: Checkpoint per level; defeat sends player back to era map, not level start
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High. Player customizes creature's 8 body parts independently, allowing stat specialization (offensive vs defensive builds). Evolution choices directly impact combat effectiveness and movement capabilities. Difficulty adapts to player's build decisions rather than scaling inversely

progression.upgrade_system: Body part evolution system: 8 sections (head, body, limbs, tail, etc.) upgraded individually via EVO points spent mid-level. Upgrades change appearance and grant new abilities (tail swings, jump height, speed). Selective evolution mid-combat allows health restoration by reallocating points
progression.unlock_mechanism: Five geological eras unlock sequentially (Cambrian/Ordovician → Carboniferous → Mesozoic → Neogene → Quaternary/Eden). Each era has map with multiple levels. Boss defeat at each level's end gates progression. Evolution options locked to current era's biology (aquatic vs mammalian vs humanoid)
replayability_hooks: Book of Life: store up to 50 unique creature designs, recall via crystals during quest
Multiple body-part upgrade combinations create different viable builds per era
Unused music and debug features suggest developer intended experimentation (hidden sound test, instant evolution to bird via debug)
Regional differences (removed feces graphics, text changes) hint at localization experimentation

world_building_techniques: Mythical framing: Gaia as Earth's embodiment and benevolent guide; Sol (Sun) as her father; Eden as ultimate reward
Geological accuracy layered with fantasy: real periods (Cambrian, Mesozoic, Ice Age) with invented creatures and timelines
Environmental storytelling: crystal anomalies signal external (Martian) interference in natural evolution; corrupted creatures dominate each age
Dialogue via crystals providing hints/lore during exploration
Final revelation: Bolbox (final boss) is single-celled organism masquerading as 'first human'—subverts creation myth expectations
Creature design reflects era: aquatic bodies for Paleozoic, dinosaur hybrids for Mesozoic, mammalian for Neogene/Quaternary

boss_design.philosophy: Culmination of each era's evolutionary arms race. Bosses are creatures corrupted by Martian crystals, representing evolutionary dead-ends or perversions. Require players to apply learned era-specific abilities and evolved body parts. Each boss contest acts as thematic climax of geological period
notable_bosses: Brosasaurus (Chapter 3, Mesozoic Era: dinosaur-analog requiring evolved combat abilities)

Bolbox (final boss, Age of Eden: massive single-celled organism controlling other lifeforms via crystals; revealed to be fraudulent 'first human'; decisive confrontation for entry into Eden and Gaia's partnership)

faction_indicators: Gaia faction (default): follow natural evolution, respect Earth's balance, seek Eden partnership
Bolbox faction (hidden/revealed): consume crystals, achieve power through corruption, dominate via monstrosity
Martian civilization (external): introduce crystals as malevolent guidance; misguide rather than nurture
danger_signals: Crystalline anomalies appearing in environments signal corrupted creatures nearby
Creatures transformed by crystals become 'monstrously powerful' and 'dominate all other lifeforms'—visual/mechanical tells of contamination
Resource overconsumption by corrupted species disrupts evolutionary flow
Bolbox's emergence as false messiah claiming to be 'first human'
environmental_storytelling: Each era's map layout reflects geological period (aquatic caverns → land forests → dinosaur-dominated plains → ice fields → humanoid settlements)
Boss arenas positioned as era climaxes: spatial progression mirrors temporal evolution
Crystals as visual anomalies scattered across landscape; their presence corrupts local fauna
Meat drops from defeated enemies signal the food chain; plant-life available for healing reinforces ecosystem
Gaia's guidance via crystals (hints, EVO grants, temporary forms) contrasts with Martian crystals (corruption)

sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor): E.V.O. debug modes, unused music, regional differences, programming details
wikipedia: Gameplay mechanics, plot synopsis, character creation, boss design, development credits, geological era framing
